texts.full_text: 13 consecutive patients (248 male, 265 female; mean age, 38 years) were operated on for 542 occurrences of Crohn's disease. Data were collected prospectively. Indications for abdominal surgery were often multiple but included failure of medical management (n = 220), obstruction (n = 94), intestinal fistul (n = 108), abscess (n = 65), bleeding (n = 29), perforation (n = 18), and cancer (n = 8). The surgical procedures performed included bowel resection (n = 410), fistulectomy (n = 65), abscess drainage (n = 65), and resection and anastomosis (n = 42). The majority of patients (n = 410) underwent bowel resection, with 312 patients (76.1%) receiving anastomosis. The ileocolic resection was the most common (n = 295), followed by ileo-ileal resection (n = 76) and ileo-ileocolic resection (n = 39). The postoperative morbidity was 15.1%, and the postoperative mortality was 1.1%. The most common postoperative complications were anastomotic leakage (n = 15) and intra-abdominal abscess (n = 13). texts.full_text: ing the interior and south central Rutland island, central interior, and south interior South Andaman Island, and the west coast of Middle Andaman Island. Järawa means "foreigners" in Aka-Bea, the language of their traditional enemies. Like many peoples, they call themselves aong, "people". The Jarawa language of the Andaman Islands is considered vulnerable. History Jarawa is a language used mainly by hunter-gatherer communities who would live primarily off resources found in dense forests and coastal areas around their territories on South and Middle Andaman Island. Historical records indicate that these people maintained relative isolation from outsiders until the late 20th century, which has helped preserve many aspects of jarawa culture including language. First contacts with British colonizers were sporadic starting as early as the mid-19<sup>th</sup> century but remained hostile for a long time. It wasn't until around 1857 that more systematic efforts to establish contact began, often leading to conflicts and disease outbreaks which severely impacted Jarawa populations. The turning point in relations between outsiders (particularly Indian settlers) and theJarawas came after an incident known as "the Great Hostility" ended with a truce established by local authorities around 1970. Since then there has been increasing interaction, although this also brings challenges such as encroachment on traditional lands. There is nothing in any of the opinions delivered in this case, either in the supreme court or the court of appeals of the state, which construes the section, in using the word “required,” as referring to any physical force being used to obtain the labor of an employee. It is assumed that the word means nothing more than the requirement arising from voluntary contract for such labor in excess of the number of hours specified in the statute. Before the Civil War Jefferson Barracks was the desired post for graduates from the military academy at West Point. The Post Exchange Building was actively utilized prior to World War I until sometime after World War II. During the decades that followed World War II, the Post Exchange started suffering from maintenance neglect and a decline in overall condition. It is also during this time that the Federal government started selling off surplus property such as equipment and land. These reductions are what lead to the purchase of much of the surrounding land by St. Louis County to create nearby Jefferson Barracks County Park. In 1988, after many years of maintenance neglect and inactive use, the Post Exchange Building along with some other property, was deeded over to the government of St. Louis County where an attempt was made to stabilize some of the structures. In 2002, the Missouri Civil War Museum not-for-profit corporation, along with the support and partnership of the St. Louis County Parks and Recreation Department, began the long and extensive historic restoration of the Post Exchange Building. Although the building became a museum and educational facility, the restoration and implementation efforts placed top priority on maintaining the historic integrity of the building and its original details. The museum contains hundreds of original artifacts from the Civil War. Visitors will see an original Stidebaker hose wagon, A Civil War cannon, a “war horse,” and many other area memorabilia. The museum contains a movie room and several other multimedia presentations. The museum welcomes groups and field trips by students. texts.full_text: 102 is adsorbed on an semiconductor 101, and a redox electrolyte 103 is infiltrated within and around the semiconductor 101. The semiconductor 101 is typically a mesoporous Tio2 film, and the dye 102 is typically a ruthenium-based dye. The redox electrolyte 103 is typically a liquid electrolyte containing iodide and triiodide. The counter electrode 104 is typically a platinum electrode, and the substrate 105 is typically a transparent conductive oxide (TCO) substrate such as a glass substrate coated with a transparent conductive oxide such as FTO (fluorine-doped tin oxide) or ITO (indium tin oxide). ADVERTISEMENT In addition, the levels of acceptable residual concentrations are increasingly restricted, which generates commercial barriers that are increasing. Besides that, and more important, are the effects of these chemicals on the environment and human health. It has been reported that fungicides generate environmental pollution due to their long periods of degradation, and they have a high toxicity and carcinogenic effects (Palou et al., 2008; Tripathi & Dubey, 2004). Alternatives to chemical control of postharvest diseases in lemons The increasing need for methods with low environmental impacts and minimal risks to human health demands the development of novel alternative solutions. Physical methods, such as heat treatment or radiation, and chemical methods using natural materials have been evaluated, but so far none proved to be effective enough to control postharvest diseases by themselves. Among the most promising tools with large potential in the control of these diseases are the strategies of biological control that are based on the use of beneficial microorganisms as natural weapons against pathogens (Wilson & Wisniewski, 1989). ADVERTISEMENT Yeasts as biocontrol agents In recent years the use of yeasts, fungi, and bacteria as biological controllers has been a promising alternative for the control of post-harvest diseases. Particularly, yeasts have certain advantages over other microorganisms: can grow rapidly in simple culture media and consequently the can be produced in bulk, they do not produce spores or allergenic toxins and have simple nutritional requirements, allowing them to colonize areas of low nutrients for long periods of time (El-Tarabily & Sivasithamparam, 2006; Druvefors et al., 2004; Qing & Shiping, 2000). Likewise, yeasts, unlike other microorganisms such as bacteria, have greater social acceptance, since their use is associated with the production of food products such as bread, or beverages such as wine and beer. texts.full_text: rises a specialized subset of small cytoplasmic noncoding RNAs between 19 and 24 nucleotides in length. miRNAs have been found in animals, plants, and viruses, with over 700 identified in humans [@pone.0016841-GriffithsJones1]. miRNA genes are first transcribed as pri-miRNAs, and then processed to shorter hairpin-shaped pre-miRNAs (∼70--90 nt) by RNase Drosha, and finally cleaved into mature single-stranded miRNAs (∼22 nt) by RNase Dicer. The processed miRNAs most frequently bind to regulatory sequences in the 3′- untranslated region (UTR) of target mRNAs through imperfect base-pairing, resulting in translational inhibition or degradation of protein-coding transcripts [@pone.0016841-Bartel1]. Bioinformatics and cloning studies have estimated that miRNAs may regulate 30% of all human genes [@pone.0016841-Lewis1] [@pone.0016841-Lim1]. Thus, miRNAs constitute a regulatory network which acts as a binary oFf-switch or a rheostat to regulate post-transcriptional gene repression in a variety of processes [@pone.0016841-bartel2] [@pone.0016841-Hornstein1]. For example, miRNAs have been implicated in various biological and pathological processes, including cell proliferation, differentiation, apoptosis, cancer development, and immune responses. The aberrant expression of miRNAs has been linked to several diseases, such as cancer, cardiovascular disease, and neurological disorders. Therefore, understanding the mechanisms and functions of miRNAs is crucial for uncovering their roles in regulating gene expression a

texts.full_text: ve model with Bayesian shrinkage This article discusses how to improve predictions for key economic indicators such as Real-Time Gross Domestic Product Growth Rate by employing advanced statistical techniques. The authors, Tucker S. McElroy and Thomas M. Trimbur from the Federal Reserve System's Board of Governors (U.S.), explore these methods in their Finance & Economics Discussion Series published back in 2012. The focus is on a particular class of models known as factor-augmented autoregressive processes which are used to predict economic variables by including additional information, or 'factors', that explain variations over and above those captured with traditional time-series approaches. These factors can be derived from various sources like financial markets data (stock prices), macroeconomic indicators such as GDP growth rates etc. The authors propose a novel Bayesian shrinkage method for the estimation of these models which allows more accurate predictions by 'shrinking' or reducing estimates towards some central value, particularly when faced with limited and noisy information. This is achieved through an iterative procedure that combines prior knowledge (Bayes’ rule) about factor loadings in conjunction with observed data to produce posterior distributions for the model parameters. texts.full_text: Our invention comprises a compact housing unit designed to be easily mounted onto the toilet bowl or positioned nearby. The device includes the following key components: 1. Motion Sensor: A motion sensor is integrated to detect the presence of a user. This sensor activates the device when the user approaches the toilet and deactivates it after a set period of inactivity. 2. Air Freshener Dispenser: An air freshener dispenser is incorporated to release a measured amount of deodorizing agent into the air. This dispenser can be refilled or replaced as needed. 3. Fan System: A small, quiet fan system is included to circulate the air, ensuring that the deodorizing agent is evenly distributed throughout the bathroom. 4. Automatic Flush Mechanism: The device is equipped with an automatic flush mechanism that triggers the toilet to flush after the user has left. This mechanism can be adjusted to flush after a predetermined delay to ensure the user has enough time to exit the area. 5. Control Unit: A control unit manages the operation of the motion sensor, air freshener dispenser, fan system, and automatic flush mechanism. This unit can be programmed to optimize the device's performance based on user preferences and bathroom conditions. 6. Power Source: The device can be powered by batteries, a rechargeable battery, or a direct power connection, depending on the user's preference and the availability of power outlets. The device operates as follows: - Detection: The motion sensor detects the user's presence and activates the device. - Deodorization: The air freshener dispenser releases the deodorizing agent, and the fan system circulates the air to eliminate foul odors. - Flushing: After the user leaves, the automatic flush mechanism triggers the toilet to flush, ensuring that the toilet contents are evacuated. - Deactivation: The device deactivates after a set period of inactivity, conserving power and ensuring efficient operation. This invention provides a simple, effective, and user-friendly solution to the problem of foul odors and the need for manual flushing in a conventional toilet. The device

texts.full_text: oticed a dramatic change in the densely forest hills below. "Look!" he says, pointing to a brown stream bisecting the velvet green landscape, "this is one of the tributaries to the Rio Tigre. This road is where the tractor goes up to cut wood. See it? It's a new road. Look there, where they take out the wood. They're not supposed to cut there, it's too close to the river." Through the windshield of his Cessna, we can see ahead of us the sumptuous forest canopy of Corcovado National Park - which is still protected from timber cutting. But directly below, loggers have been looting the forest reserve surrounding the park, which provides critical additional habitat. The hilltops are checkered with clearcuts that look like soccer fields; from them lead logging roads, "ant trails," he calls them. We turn around and head for the airstrip in the trading center of Puerto Jimenez. Ramirez stares ahead, a look of resignation on his face. "They're clear-cutting the watersheds," he says. The Osa Peninsula is one of the largest expanses of lowland tropical forests left in Central America. Jutting off of the Pacific Coast just above Panama, the Osa is Costa Rican exceptional haven for biodiversity and home to more than half a million acres (210 hectares) protected land between national parks such as Corcovado National Park, Piedras Blancas Reserve Area Reforestadas of Sabalito Wildlife Refuge. The aesthetic designs are typically implemented by printing or otherwise providing graphics on one (or more) of component members of those disposable absorbent articles (e.g., a backsheet). It is believed that such aesthetic features can provide emotional benefit to users, and thus, for example, in the case of sanitary napkins, may decrease users' mood during the menstruation period. In diaper embodiments, the graphics may provide the diaper wearer or caregiver with a pleasant or interesting design. Some disposable absorbent articles (typically sanitary napkins and pantiliners) are folded and wrapped individually by a wrapper sheet. The materials of such wrapper members are typically polyethylene films or sometimes nonwoven materials which are not necessarily transparent. In some cases, this is intentional because consumers of such disposable absorbent articles do not want other people to notice or show they are carrying the articles, such as sanitary napkins. Also, in some cases, the wrapper sheet is used for disposal of the used product and, as such, a non-transparent wrapper can prevent the soiled article from being seen through the wrapper sheet. texts.full_text: Jones is speaking to a twentysomething interviewer who knows enough to profile the real-life prominent AIDS and LGBT activist, but seems otherwise woefully undereducated about a struggle he’s inherited and that jones has lived through. The older man gently chides him—and, by extension, his generation—for his ignorance, but then patiently tells the kid a story more than 40 years in the making, that nevertheless has many chapters still unwritten. Advertisement A similar dynamic occurs on the macro level of When We Rise, which patiently offers context for any and every viewer. texts.preview_tail: l and am a total foodie ! I love making lavender wands and cooking and sewing and gardening and preserving and of course quilting and blogging ! I love to hear from you - comments, questions, advice !
texts.full_text: a chubby, middle aged, wife to the love of my life for 30 years and mommy to three amazing young men. We live in an 1850's Victorian on a hill - lavender of course ! She is a constant work in progress and even after 25 yearsshe's still showing us things we never knew she had ! We live in the Adirondack Park and I am grateful everyday to live in such a beautiful place ! With all the wildlife around I never know what I'll see in my backyard or even out my kitchen window ! I am a small business owner, I make all kinds of things with lavender, I sell in local shops, a local lavender farm and through my online store. I LOVE to read and travel and am a total foodie ! I love making lavender wands and cooking and sewing and gardening and preserving and of course quilting and blogging ! I love to hear from you - comments, questions, advice !
